content.title: Partition
content.content:
A **partition** of $[a, b]$ is a finite set 
$P = \{x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ with
\[
a = x_0 < x_1 < x_2 < \cdots < x_n = b.
\]
The **norm** or **mesh** of $P$ is 
$\|P\| = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} (x_i - x_{i-1})$.

content.title: Riemann Sum
content.content:
Given a partition $P$ and a bounded function $f$ on $[a, b]$, a 
**Riemann sum** is any sum of the form
\[
S(f, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f(t_i)(x_i - x_{i-1})
\]
where $t_i \in [x_{i-1}, x_i]$ is a **sample point** in each subinterval.

content.title: Upper and Lower Sums
content.content:
For a bounded function $f$ on $[a, b]$ and partition $P$, define:
$$
M_i &= \sup\{f(x) : x \in [x_{i-1}, x_i]\} \\
m_i &= \inf\{f(x) : x \in [x_{i-1}, x_i]\}
$$
The **upper sum** is $U(f, P) = \sum_{i=1}^n M_i(x_i - x_{i-1})$.

The **lower sum** is $L(f, P) = \sum_{i=1}^n m_i(x_i - x_{i-1})$.

content.text: For any Riemann sum with partition $P$: $L(f, P) \leq S(f, P) \leq U(f, P)$.

content.title: Refinement Lemma
content.content:
If $Q$ is a refinement of $P$ (i.e., $P \subseteq Q$), then
\[
L(f, P) \leq L(f, Q) \leq U(f, Q) \leq U(f, P).
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Adding a single point to $P$ cannot decrease the lower sum (we take inf 
over smaller sets) or increase the upper sum. Apply inductively.

content.title: Upper and Lower Integrals
content.content:
The **upper integral** and **lower integral** are
$$
\overline{\int_a^b} f &= \inf\{U(f, P) : P \text{ is a partition of } [a,b]\} \\
\underline{\int_a^b} f &= \sup\{L(f, P) : P \text{ is a partition of } [a,b]\}
$$

content.title: Upper Integral $\geq$ Lower Integral
content.content:
For any bounded function $f$ on $[a, b]$:
\[
\underline{\int_a^b} f \leq \overline{\int_a^b} f.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For any partitions $P$ and $Q$, let $R = P \cup Q$. Then 
$L(f, P) \leq L(f, R) \leq U(f, R) \leq U(f, Q)$. Thus every lower sum 
is $\leq$ every upper sum. Taking sup and inf gives the result.

content.title: Riemann Integrable
content.content:
A bounded function $f$ on $[a, b]$ is **Riemann integrable** 
if $\underline{\int_a^b} f = \overline{\int_a^b} f$. In this case, the common 
value is denoted $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ or $\int_a^b f$.
